document_text:
Why Large Language Models can Secretly Outperform Embedding
Similarity in Information Retrieval
Matei Benescu
University of Groningen
matei.benescu22@gmail.comIvo Pascal de Jong
University of Groningen
ivo.de.jong@rug.nl
Abstract
With the emergence of Large Language Models
(LLMs), new methods in Information Retrieval
are available in which relevance is estimated
directly through language understanding and
reasoning, instead of embedding similarity. We
argue that similarity is ashort-sightedinterpre-
tation of relevance, and that LLM-Based Rel-
evance Judgment Systems (LLM-RJS) (with
reasoning) have potential to outperform Neu-
ral Embedding Retrieval Systems (NERS) by
overcoming this limitation. Using the TREC-
DL 2019 passage retrieval dataset, we compare
various LLM-RJS with NERS, but observe no
noticeable improvement. Subsequently, we an-
alyze the impact of reasoning by comparing
LLM-RJS with and without reasoning. We
find that human annotations also suffer from
short-sightedness, and that false-positives in the
reasoning LLM-RJS are primarily mistakes in
annotations due to short-sightedness. We con-
clude that LLM-RJS do have the ability to ad-
dress the short-sightedness limitation in NERS,
but that this cannot be evaluated with standard
annotated relevance datasets.
1 Introduction
Information Retrieval (IR) is the task of finding
relevant information in a pool of numerous docu-
ments, where a document can be any retrievable
unit like a webpage or text fragment. IR meth-
ods have constantly been evolving (Kazi et al.,
2025). Vector Space Models (Salton, 1965; Salton
et al., 1975) use sparse vector representations of
term weights, where each weight represents the
frequency of the term in the given document. Doc-
uments are ordered by relevance to the query, by the
means of an ordering function, such as the BM25
(Robertson et al., 2009, Chapter 3.4). Modern IR
methods rely on dense vector representations of
text, known as “embeddings", which are learned
by Neural Networks (Huang et al., 2013). We will
query:
difference between
mcdouble and double
cheeseburger
document:
one slice of cheese
instead of two. It's
twenty cents more.Embedding
Similarity
Relevance
Reasoning✅🚫Do these match?Figure 1: Motivation for LLM-RJS. The query and
document are not similar, so embedding similarity is too
short-sighted for IR. The document is the correct answer,
but requires reasoning about relevance to identify as a
match.
refer to these methods as Neural Embeddings Re-
trieval Systems (NERS). In NERS, documents are
ordered by their similarity with the query, which is
given by a distance metric such as Euclidean Dis-
tance, or Cosine Similarity (Wang and Dong, 2020).
Karpukhin et al., 2020 showed that NERS outper-
form sparse vector representation approaches.
More recently, Large Language Models (LLMs)
have been proposed as a different approach to judg-
ing how relevant a document is to a query (MacA-
vaney and Soldaini, 2023; Faggioli et al., 2023;
Abbasiantaeb et al., 2024). Alaofi et al., 2024
showed that LLMs’ relevance judgments are sim-
ilar to those of humans. This has the potential to
outperform NERS models, as NERS models are
limited by retrieving documents that aresimilarto
the query, but relevant documents are not always
similar. An example of NERS being short-sighted
is shown in Figure 1.
For the scope of this paper, we will call a sys-
tem that uses an LLMs verbalized relevance score
for matching anLLM-Based Relevance Judgment
System(LLM-RJS). While LLM-RJS have been
1arXiv:2603.08077v1  [cs.IR]  9 Mar 2026

shown to be effective (Alaofi et al., 2024), to the
best of our knowledge a 1-to-1 comparison against
NERS is missing. Additionally, IR applications of
reasoning models that are equipped with Chain of
Thought (CoT) capabilities, such as GPT-o1 (Jaech
et al., 2024) remain unexplored. As reasoning ca-
pabilities become cheaper and available in open
models (Guo et al., 2025), examining its impact on
document relevance judgments becomes increas-
ingly important and broadly applicable. We aim
to build on Alaofi et al., 2024 by comparing LLM-
RJS to NERS. Furthermore, we explore the effects
of reasoning capabilities of LLMs in the task of
document relevance estimation.
With this, we make the following contributions:
•We evaluate NERS and multiple LLM-RJS
models on the TREC-DL 2019 passage re-
trieval benchmark to compare their perfor-
mances in relevance ranking. We see that top
LLMs perform on par with NERS, but do not
yield demonstrably better rankings.
•We evaluate the impact of “reasoning effort”
for LLM-RJS at directly estimating the rele-
vance scores. We propose that this can help to
overcome the short-sightedness of NERS. We
find that adding reasoning results in higher
relevance ratings that the human relevance
scores, and that the LLM-RJS overall shows
higher relevance ratings.
•We explain the difference in relevance ratings
as short-sightedness in the ground-truth anno-
tations, showing examples of documents that
have low relevance ratings in the human an-
notation, but that are highly relevant. From
the 94 ‘false positives’, 89 are actually mis-
takes in the annotation because the passages
are relevant but not similar.
•We conclude that the empirical capabilities of
zero-shot methods for Information Retrieval
may be underestimated due to short-sighted
annotations, making short-sighted similarity
models appear better than more capable rea-
soning models. LLM-RJS have the ability
to overcome the short-sighted limitation of
NERS, but this does not lead to better metrics
when evaluated on human annotations.
2 Related Work
We describe previous research on NERS and LLM-
RJS models. While NERS stem from a long line
of existing research in text embeddings (Mikolovet al., 2013; Pennington et al., 2014), we specifi-
cally focus on embeddings from Neural Networks.
NERSNeural Embedding Retrieval Systems
(NERS) rely on pre-trained language models to
create a dense vector representation of text. These
language models are Neural Networks trained on
some other such as Masked Language Modeling
(Devlin et al., 2019) or Contrastive Learning (Gao
et al., 2021). The penulatimate layer of the Neural
Network therefore has a dense representation of the
semantic meaning of the text, capturing properties
like synonymy (Karpukhin et al., 2020).
NERS models work by computing the embed-
ding representation of the query and the document,
and evaluating the distance between them, typically
through cosine similarity. When a document and a
query describe similar concepts they get a higher
similarity score. Document relevance is therefore
ranked by similarity to the query.
We consider this a fundamental limitation of IR
systems that rely on embedding similarities. While
similarity between query and document is a good
heuristic for relevance, it is not exactly the same.
When the document answers the question but does
not repeat content from the query (as the example
in Figure 1), it is not similar, but can still be highly
relevant. Even with ever-improving embedding
models (Trabelsi et al., 2021), this necessarily gives
a limitation of NERS.
LLM-RJSLarge Language Model-Based Rele-
vance Judgement Systems (LLM-RJS) instead use
the generated answers from an LLM to establish
relevance. In this work, the LLM is prompted with
query and document, and instructed to quantify
how “relevant” the document is in line with the
human annotations, though many variations for
prompting exist (Arabzadeh and Clarke, 2025).
Faggioli et al., 2023 found that LLMs can per-
form comparably to humans in identifying irrele-
vant information but struggled to consistently rec-
ognize relevant pieces of information. In their re-
sults GPT-3.5 showed a higher false negative rate
than humans.
Evaluating the goodness of document relevance
assigned by LLMs can be directly compared to
human-labeled query-document pairs are used as
the “ground truth". However, humans are not con-
sistent in rating document relevance (Bailey et al.,
2008). To evaluate LLMs fairly, Alaofi et al., 2024
posed the issue as “if LLMs produce labels that
agree with humans to the same extent that humans

have seen goldfish grow ridiculously large in very small containers when their water was
changed frequently. Goldfish will not grow if water conditions are poor. Fancy goldfish
don’t grow as large as Common goldfish. A good size would be around 5 inches body
length for most fancy varieties, 8 inches for Comets and 12 inches for Common Goldfish.
These sizes are usually only attained by pond grown fish.3
Table 1: Examples from TREC-DL 2019 test set. Relevance: 0 = Irrelevant, 1 = Related, 2 = Highly relevant, 3 =
Perfectly relevant.
agree with each other (human-to-human range), we
can conclude that they are sufficiently reliable for
use". Their results show that larger LLMs, such as
GPT-4, GPT-4o, and LLaMA70B give results in the
human-to-human range. Only GPT-4 and GPT-4o
were robust to the prompt variations. However, this
perspective cannot show when LLMs might outper-
form humans in the ability to estimate relevance.
Because LLM-RJS systems are zero-shot models
and are not trained as supervised learning systems
on the human annotations, we should consider that
it has the potential to outperform human annota-
tions. Additionally, with reasoning models (Wei
et al., 2022), we can generate reasoning processes
that show relevance in a way that has more depth
than simple similarity scores, and might have more
depth than the human labels.
3 Dataset
TREC (Text REtrieval Conference) datasets are
commonly used in the IR field (MacAvaney and
Soldaini, 2023; Faggioli et al., 2023; Alaofi et al.,
2024). Similarly to Alaofi et al., 2024, we use
queries and passages from the test set of the the
passage retrieval task dataset of the Deep Learning
Track of TREC 2019 (TREC-DL 2019) (V oorhees
et al., 2020). TREC-DL 2019 contains triples of
form (query, passage, relevance score).
The passage corpora of TREC-DL 2019 are de-
rived from the MS MARCO dataset. MS MARCO
is a large-scale dataset of real web search queries
and web text originally sampled from Bing search
logs. Queries in MS MARCO are real user queriesand passages are extracted from web documents ob-
tained via Bing search results. Table 1 gives some
examples of (query, passage, relevance) triples.
The relevance scores of TREC-DL 2019 are eval-
uated by human assessors. The scores (V oorhees
et al., 2020, Section 2.2) are integer values using a
4-point scale (0-3) where:
3.Perfectly relevant:The passage is dedicated
to the query and contains the exact answer.
2.Highly relevant:The passage has some an-
swer for the query, but the answer may be
a bit unclear, or hidden amongst extraneous
information.
1.Related:The passage seems related to the
query but does not answer it.
0.Irrelevant:The passage has nothing to do
with the query.
For both experiments, the LLMs are instructed to
only output the relevance score of the (query, docu-
ment) pair. Any response that could not be casted
to an integer between 0 and 3 was considered a
failed response. All others are successes. For each
model, the (query, document) pairs were divided
into successes and failures. The intersection of all
successful pairs was kept for final analysis.
4 Experiment 1
To evaluate whether LLM-RJS can outperform
NERS, and therefore attempt to address the short-
sightedness issue of NERS, we compare the perfor-
mance of NERS with LLM-RJS in document rank-
ing. As a baseline we evaluate the BM25 model.
We first describe the implementation details of the

three models, and then how they are evaluated. The
overall setup of Experiment 1 is demonstrated in
Figure 2.
LLM-RJSAll models were asked to estimate
the relevance of (query, passage) pairs on a 4-point
scale pair, using the prompt found in Appendix A.
The prompt describes the task and provides the rel-
evance score convention. The models were also
given examples of (query, document, relevance)
triples, as examples are known to enhance LLM
performance (Brown et al., 2020). We use both
closed state-of-the-art (at the moment of writing),
as well as open-weights models. Table 2 provides
an overview of the LLM models evaluated. The
temperature hyperparameter (Ackley et al., 1985;
Peeperkorn et al., 2024), controls the amount of
“randomness” in LLM token generation. The tem-
perature was set to 0 for all models, to ensure re-
producibility and because higher temperatures have
been shown to have a moderate correlation with in-
coherence (Peeperkorn et al., 2024). The reasoning
efforts of GPT-5.1 was set to “none”. The open-
weights models were run using Ollama1.
1https://ollama.com/Neural Embeddings Retrieval Systems:For
NERS, we used a closed model and an open-
weights one. The closed model, gemini-
embedding001, is the state-of-the-art from Google,
showed to outperform most of the current embed-
ding models (Lee et al., 2025). The open model
is nomic-embed-text:v1.52. It is an extension of
nomic-embed-text:v1 (Nussbaum et al., 2024) that
uses Matryoshka Representation Learning (Kusu-
pati et al., 2022). The base model, nomic-embed-
text:v1, showed better performance (Nussbaum
et al., 2024) than similarly sized embedding mod-
els and established models like GloVe (Pennington
et al., 2014).
BM25 baseline:BM25 is a probabilistic scoring
function that ranks documents based on query term
frequencies while balancing the effects of term fre-
quency and document length.
The BM25 scoring function is defined as:
BM25(d, q) =P

frequency saturation, |d|is the length of document
d,avgdl is the average document length in the col-
lection, and b∈[0,1] controls the strength of doc-

The inverse document frequency (IDF) reduces
the weight of common terms while increasing the
importance of rarer terms across the collection:
IDF(t) = logN−n t+ 0.5

LLM-RJS models perform on par with NERS models, but do not appear to outperform them. Results are in line
with previous research from Hofstätter et al. (2021); Xing et al. (2023), reporting mean NDCG@10 of 0.72 and 0.73
respectively.
Similarly to V oorhees et al., 2020, BM25 with
the default parameters ( k1= 1.5 ,b= 0.75 ) was
chosen as a baseline ranking system. The rank-
ings yielded by both LLM-RJS and NERS will be
compared to the one of BM25.
Model evaluation:The relevance estimates from
NERS and BM25 cannot be directly compared to
ground truth relevance scores, due to different rep-
resentations of relevance. Instead, the document
rankings, i.e. the set of documents ordered by their
relevance to the query, yielded by the different sys-
tems can be directly compared.
The Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain
(NDCG) (Wang et al., 2013) is a measure of rank-
ing quality, which we will use to compare the rank-
ings obtained by different retrieval systems.
NDCG is defined as:
NDCG(A) =DCG(A)
DCG(I),
DCG(R) =NX
i=1h(di)
log(i+ 1),
where Iis the ideal ranking and h(di)is the rele-
vance of document dat position iin the ranking.
NDCG ranges from 0 to 1, with 1 corresponding
to the ideal ranking induced by human relevance
judgments.
NDCG@k is NDCG evaluated for the sub-ranking
that consists of the first kdocuments in the initial
ranking.
Since each query yields a ranking, and the
dataset consists of multiple queries, the average
NDCG and NDCG@k with k= 5 andk= 10over queries will be computed as final evaluation
metrics.
4.1 Results and Discussion
For each query, all the passages in relation with it
have been ranked, and the NDCG, NDCG@5 and
NDCG@10 has been computed for the rank yielded
by each model. Figure 3 shows the results of Ex-
periment 1 as a violin plot with NDCG, NDCG@5
and NDCG@10 of all models.
The NERS and LLM-RJS models performed on
par with previous models Hofstätter et al., 2021;
Xing et al., 2023. These previous models are pur-
posely trained dense retrievers, unlike the mod-
els analyzed by this paper, which are zero-shot.
Note that the zero-shot models seem to outperform
trained retrievers. The presented NDCG@10 of
these previous models was reported in the respec-
tive papers and not implemented here.
For this experiment, only Llama3.1:8b,
Llama3.2:1b and Sonnet 4.5 had any failures,
out of which, Llama3.2:1bs and Sonnet 4.5 have
been excluded for having failed more than 2/3 of
all data points (lack of instruction compliance,
see Appendix A. Table 5 provides an example of
failure for each model.). The models failed to only
output one integer between 0 and 3. Expectedly,
one of the excluded models is Llama3.2:1b, the
smallest model in the selection, with 8312/9260
failed responses. Surprisingly, the other one
was Sonnet 4.5, the state-of-the-art (at the time
of writing) of Anthropic, which failed to only
give relevance scores for 7473/9260 data points.
Llama3.1:8b only failed 84/9260 data points.
Overall, NERS are still most proficient for rank-

ing documents, and perform the task better for only
a fraction of the cost, and a fraction of the time. The
price per 1 million tokens strongly favors NERS,
with Gemini Embeddings costing 0.15$ per 1 mil-
lion tokens. On the other side, GPT-5.1 costs 1.25$
per 1 million input tokens and 10$ per 1 million out-
put tokens. Albeit financially undesirable, LLMs
do not leave much on the table, when it comes to
ranking (query, document) pairs.
The closed-source models consistently outper-
form the open models. The bottom line is, if one is
looking for the best option, Gemini Embeddings is
the winning model. If, aside performance, running
the retrieval system locally is also a consideration,
nomic-embed-text:v1.5 is a viable option.
5 Experiment 2
Chain of Thought (CoT) reasoning (Wei et al.,
2022) has been shown to yield substantial improve-
ment in LLM capabilities on a variety of tasks. It
works by letting the LLM generate a longer series
of tokens as “reasoning” before giving the final an-
swer. This strategy has been shown to lead to better
results in image Information Retrieval (Wu et al.,
2025), and is therefore also promising for having
LLM-RJS outperform NERS models.
We prompt GPT-5.1 to estimate the relevance
of (document, query) pairs with the prompt de-
scribed in Appendix A. To compare the reasoning
against the non-reasoning conditions we change
the “reasoning effort”3, which controls the amount
of reasoning tokens before the output. A visual
overview of the experiment is shown in Figure 4.
The same experiment was also performed with
gpt-oss:20b, a smaller open-source model, with
3https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/reasoning#get-
started-with-reasoningsimilar results reported in Appendix C.
Since the LLM-RJS can directly estimate prompt
relevance under the same definitions as the human
annotations, the relevance estimates are compared
directly, instead of by the ranking. Figure 5 shows
the confusion matrices of the non-reasoning and
reasoning LLM-RJS relevance rating compared to
human relevance ratings.
We observe reasonable agreement between the
LLM-RJS systems and the human annotations,
but find that in many cases the LLM-RJS assigns
higher relevance ratings than the humans anno-
tations. This is particularly visible in the large
number of cases where the human rating has rel-
evance 0 (irrelevant), while the LLM-RJS has rel-
evance 1 (related). This difference increases for
the reasoning model, which identifies yet more
(document, query) pairs as related. These higher
ratings are also reflected in the average relevance
ratings, where the average human rating is 0.79,
LLM-RJS without reasoning is 1.03, and with rea-
soning is 1.23.

Table 3: First 10 query-IDs where GPT5.1 gave rating 3 while the humans gave 0. The annotations suffer from
short-sightedness, while GPT-5.1 with reasoning correctly identifies relevance. Of the 94 such cases only 5 were
genuine false positives (shown in Appendix B). The NERS similarity averages 0.44 for these 94 cases, while it is
0.50 for true positives (higher is more similar).Boldhighlights key examples of the short-sightedness limitation.
Particularly interesting is that the reasoning con-
figuration, which would be expected to perform bet-
ter, has double the incidences where human ratings
identify a pair as irrelevant (0), while the LLM-RJS
identifies the pair as perfectly relevant.
Table 3 shows examples from these cases. Pre-
dominantly, the passages are actually highly rele-
vant, but might not be immediately obvious. The
“difference between a mcdouble and a double
cheeseburger” is indeed one slice of cheese instead
of two. This may not be obvious to human anno-
tators or short-sighted NERS models that evaluate
similarity, but in these cases the reasoning LLM-
RJS outperforms the benchmark. Overall the re-
sults from Experiment 2 show that the addition of
reasoning allows the model to identify relevance
that is not obvious, beyond the human annotations.6 Conclusion
We proposed that the short-sightedness problem of
NERS may be resolved with relevance reasoning
LLM-RJS. Similiarity is not always sufficient for
relevance. In Experiment 1 we evaluated whether
these LLM-RJS outperform NERS on a ranking
task, and do not find improvement. In Experiment
2 we added reasoning, and directly compared the
LLM-RJS relevance rankings to the human rank-
ings. This showed the the LLM-RJS was able to
identify more documents as relevant than the hu-
man annotations. This suggests that the human
annotations suffer from similar short-sightedness
as the NERS systems, explaining why the LLM-
RJS do not reach a noticeable improvement com-
pared to NERS when evaluated on NDCG with
short-sighted human annotations as ground truth.
7

LimitationsThe primary limitation to LLM-RJS
systems is the added cost compared to embedding
models. The price-per-token of top performing
LLMs is roughly 10x that of equivalent embedding
models. Additionally, for NERS systems imple-
mented at scale, each document only needs to be
embedded once even for multiple queries, and all
document embeddings can be stored in a vector
database for efficient retrieval. The LLM-RJS sys-
tem demonstrated here requires LLM inference for
each (query, document) pair and therefore does not
scale well. A solution may be found in CoT re-
ranking (Wu et al., 2025), where an embedding
model is used to make a first selection of docu-
ments that are similar, and then the reasoning-LLM
selects the relevant documents from the subset of
similar documents. However, this only addresses
the problem where similar documents are not rel-
evant, but would still fail in cases as the example
in Figure 1, where the document is relevant but not
similar.
Additionally, we should consider that the short-
sightedness in human annotations is not universal
for all datasets. We have shown cases of short-
sightedness in this dataset, but through expert an-
notators, instructions that support further research,
and more time for annotations it is possible to con-
struct relevance annotations that do not suffer from
short-sightedness. Since LLM-RJS models do not
need large training datasets, efforts can be focused
on only creating test sets.
Future workThe presented results suggest, but
do not definitively show that LLM-RJS models
can outperform human annotations. To establish
the performance difference between human rele-
vance and LLM-RJS relevance re-annotation may
be done where trained annotators compare exist-
ing labels and LLM-RJS relevance judgements and
establish which is correct. This can be supported
by additionally providing the reasoning chain from
the LLM-RJS, to provide argumentation for the
judgment.
We have established in this instance of an In-
formation Retrieval a mismatch between similarity
and relevance, and show this as a nearsightedness-
bottleneck for embeddings. However, for a variety
of different Information Retrieval tasks, a variety
of retrieval requirements exist beyond ‘relevance’.
In tasks further conditions apply such as prefer-
ring high-quality documents, while in other tasks
specifically similarity might be preferred over rele-vance (e.g. for detecting copyright infringement).
With advanced Information Retrieval capabilities
of LLM-RJS models, soft requirements can be con-
sidered, and there are more possibilities to deviate
from ‘similarity’ or requirements that can be quan-
tified. Evaluating LLM-RJS against human anno-
tations still appears to be the standard (Arabzadeh
and Clarke, 2025), and efforts are needed to re-
specify what the (sub)tasks of Information Re-
trieval problems are, and how LLM-RJS models
can be evaluated.
In conclusion, we found that the short-
sightedness motivation presented in the illustra-
tive Figure 1 is a valid limitation of NERS models
that can be addressed by LLM-RJS. However, as
shown with the examples of short-sighted annota-
tions in Table 3, human annotations suffer from
the same short-sightedness limitation, they present
as false-positives for a confusion matrix in Figure
5. Therefore, under a standard annotated bench-
mark evaluation as shown in Figure 3, they do not
demonstrably outperform NERS, while they still
could be giving better relevance judgments.
Declaration on Generative AI:For this paper,
OpenAi’s GPT-5, GPT-5.1 and GPT-5.2 models
were used for minor reformulations and spelling
checking, and LaTeX formatting.

A Prompt
The prompt that was used for all LLMs was:
You are an automatic passage relevance judge for information retrieval.
Your task is to rate how relevant a single passage is to a given query.
Follow these instructions:
- Carefully read the query and the passage.
- Decide how relevant the passage is to the query only, ignoring any external knowledge you might have.
- Assign exactly one integer relevance score using this 4-point scale:
3 - Perfectly relevant:
The passage is clearly dedicated to the query and contains the exact answer or all key information
needed to answer it. No important part of the answer is missing. ,→
Example:
Query: "What is chocolate?"
Passage: "Chocolate is a food made by roasting and grinding cacao seeds."
Score: 3
2 - Highly relevant:
The passage contains some answer to the query, but the answer may be partially unclear, incomplete,
indirect, or mixed with a lot of extraneous information. Still, it provides clear help in
answering the query.,→
,→
Example:
Query: "Labrador breed"
Passage: "Labradors are friendly dogs often used as guide animals."
Score: 2
(Passage is about Labradors but doesn't define the breed fully.)
1 - Related:
The passage is about a topic clearly related to the query, but it does not actually answer the query.
It may provide background, context, or tangential information only. ,→
Example:
Query: "What is chocolate?"
Passage: "Cacao trees grow in tropical
climates."
Score: 1 (Related ingredient, but no definition of chocolate.)
0 - Irrelevant:
The passage has nothing to do with the query or is only extremely weakly related, and it does not help
answer the query at all.,→
Example:
Query: "Labrador breed"
Passage: "The Eiffel Tower is in Paris."
Score: 0
Output format:
You must always return a single integer score between 0 and 3 and NOTHING else.
11

Table 4: The 5 false positives where GPT 5.1 with reasoning gave a relevance rating of 3, while the human
annotations gave a relevance of 0. While many passages are partially relevant, we believe they do not fully answer
the query and should receive a lower rating. All other 89 ‘false positives’ are due to mistakes in the annotations, not
mistakes of the LLM-RJS.
C Reasoning experiment with GPT-OSS
In Figure 6 we reproduce Experiment 2 with an open-source reasoning model. Again we see that the
model is more likely to give higher relevance ratings than the humans, and that this effect is larger for
the reasoning model. Compared to GPT 5.1, we do see an increase in false-negatives (lower triangular)
suggesting that this model might also have generally increased errors.

D Examples of LLM-RJS failure
LLM Query Passage LLM output
Sonnet 4.5 anthropological defini-
tion of environmentMan and environment reciprocal re-
lationship. The relationship between
humans and environment has varied
from the early periods of human set-
tlement on the earth to the present day.
The relationship between environment
and human beings has also being vary-
ing from place to place at any given
period of time.1
The passage discusses the relationship
between humans and environment
from an anthropological perspective,
which is related to the query topic.
However, it does not actually provide a
definition of “environment” in anthro-
pological terms. It only describes how
the human–environment relationship
varies across time and space, which is
contextual information rather than a
definition.

to complete.3 To get an exact price for

paint can retail for $20 to $40. 2 To

llama3.1:8b do goldfish grow The dolphin fish is the fastest grow-
ing wild fish species known to man.
When the conditions are right, the
dolphin fish can grow as fast as 1.3 to
2.7 inches in a week!That means that
in about 1 year, the fish can grow to
four feet long and could be as heavy
as 40 pounds. Studies have shown
that females rarely go over 40 pounds
while males can grow to as much as 60
pounds. As a fast-growing fish species,
it is also among those which mature
early. In fact, dolphin fish are sexually
mature in as early as 3 months, some
even maturing at just 14 inches long.You want me to rate the relevance of
the passage to the query “do goldfish
grow".
After carefully reading both the query
and the passage, I assign a score of:
Score: 2
The passage provides clear informa-
tion about the growth rate of dolphin
fish, including their potential size and
weight. The passage also mentions that
studies have shown females rarely ex-
ceed 40 pounds while males can reach
up to 60 pounds, which is relevant to
the query.
However, the passage does not directly
answer whether goldfish grow or not.
It provides general information about
dolphin fish’s growth rate but does not
provide any direct evidence or exam-
ples of goldfish growing. Therefore, I
assign a score of 2 instead of 3, indi-
cating that the passage is related to the
query but does not provide a definitive
answer.

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the potential of Large Language Models (LLMs) to outperform Neural Embedding Retrieval Systems (NERS) in Information Retrieval tasks. The main points covered include the limitation of NERS in retrieving relevant documents due to their reliance on embedding similarity, and the potential of LLM-Based Relevance Judgment Systems (LLM-RJS) to overcome this limitation by using language understanding and reasoning to estimate relevance. The document also presents a comparison of LLM-RJS and NERS on the TREC-DL 2019 passage retrieval dataset, and analyzes the impact of reasoning on document relevance judgments. Additionally, it highlights the issue of short-sightedness in human annotations, which can lead to underestimation of the capabilities of LLM-RJS. Overall, the document explores the potential of LLMs to improve Information Retrieval tasks by addressing the limitations of traditional embedding-based approaches.

structure.title: Related Work
structure.summary: This partial document discusses the comparison of Neural Embedding Retrieval Systems (NERS) and Large Language Model-Based Relevance Judgement Systems (LLM-RJS) in the task of document relevance estimation. The main points covered include the limitations of NERS, which rely on similarity between query and document embeddings, and the potential of LLM-RJS to overcome these limitations by using generated answers from large language models to establish relevance. The document also presents the results of experiments comparing the performance of NERS and LLM-RJS on the TREC-DL 2019 passage retrieval benchmark, including the evaluation of the impact of "reasoning effort" on LLM-RJS and the analysis of the differences in relevance ratings between human annotations and LLM-RJS. Additionally, the document discusses the potential of LLM-RJS to outperform human annotations in estimating relevance and the importance of considering the capabilities of zero-shot models in information retrieval tasks.

structure.title: Dataset
structure.summary: The partial document covers various topics, including the definition of axon terminals and synaptic knobs, declaratory judgments in patent cases, the difference between a McDouble and a Double Cheeseburger, and the growth of goldfish. It also discusses the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) in estimating relevance scores for query-passage pairs, comparing their performance to human annotations and other models such as Neural Embeddings Retrieval Systems (NERS) and BM25. The document provides an overview of Experiment 1, which evaluates the performance of LLMs in document ranking, and lists the LLM models used in the experiment. Additionally, it explains the implementation details of the models, including the prompt used for LLMs, the temperature hyperparameter, and the BM25 scoring function. The document also mentions the use of the TREC-DL 2019 dataset and the evaluation metrics used to compare the performance of the models.

structure.title: Experiment 1
structure.summary: The partial document discusses an experiment to evaluate the performance of various language models and neural embedding retrieval systems in ranking documents based on their relevance to a given query. The experiment involves comparing the rankings produced by these models with those produced by human labelers, using metrics such as Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG). The document describes the setup of the experiment, the models used, and the results, which show that the top-performing language models are on par with the neural embedding retrieval systems, but do not outperform them. The document also discusses the evaluation metrics used and the challenges faced by some of the models in producing valid relevance scores. Overall, the experiment aims to assess the effectiveness of different models in retrieving relevant documents in response to a query.

nodes.title: Results and Discussion
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the performance of various language models, including LLM-RJS and NERS, in relevance ranking tasks. The main points covered include the comparison of these models using the Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) metric, the evaluation of their performance in ranking documents, and the analysis of their strengths and weaknesses. The document also explores the use of Chain of Thought (CoT) reasoning to improve the capabilities of LLMs and presents the results of experiments comparing the performance of LLM-RJS models with and without reasoning. Additionally, the document touches on the financial and computational costs of using different models, with NERS models being more cost-effective, and the performance of closed-source models versus open models, with the former consistently outperforming the latter. Overall, the document provides an in-depth analysis of the performance of various language models in relevance ranking tasks and explores ways to improve their capabilities.

structure.title: Experiment 2
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of document relevance using Large Language Models (LLMs) and their comparison with human assessments. It presents the results of two experiments, one comparing the performance of LLMs with and without reasoning capabilities, and another evaluating the cost-effectiveness of different models. The main points covered include the ability of LLMs to estimate relevance of document-query pairs, the impact of reasoning on LLM performance, and the comparison of LLMs with other models such as Gemini Embeddings and NERS. The document also highlights the differences between human and LLM relevance ratings, with LLMs tend to assign higher relevance ratings, especially when using reasoning capabilities.

nodes.title: Chain of Thought (CoT) reasoning
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of document relevance using Large Language Models (LLMs) with and without reasoning capabilities. The experiment compares the performance of LLMs with human annotations and Neural Embedding Retrieval Systems (NERS) in ranking query-document pairs. The results show that LLMs with reasoning capabilities can identify more relevant documents than human annotations, suggesting that human annotations may suffer from short-sightedness. The document also highlights the limitations of NERS models in evaluating similarity and relevance, and proposes the use of LLMs with reasoning as a potential solution to overcome these limitations. The experiment evaluates the performance of LLMs in two conditions: with and without reasoning, and compares the results to human annotations and NERS models. The findings suggest that LLMs with reasoning capabilities can outperform NERS models and human annotations in identifying relevant documents, especially in cases where the relevance is not immediately obvious.

nodes.title: Comparison of LLM-RJS with and without reasoning
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the limitations of human annotations and Named Entity Recognition Systems (NERS) in evaluating relevance, and proposes the use of Large Language Models with Relevance and Joint Semantics (LLM-RJS) to improve relevance ranking. It highlights the "short-sightedness" problem of NERS and human annotations, where they may not always identify relevant information. The document presents the results of two experiments, showing that LLM-RJS can outperform NERS and human annotations in identifying relevant documents, particularly in cases where the relevance is not immediately obvious. The main points covered include the definition and limitations of NERS, the concept of short-sightedness in human annotations, and the potential of LLM-RJS to improve relevance ranking.

structure.title: Conclusion
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the limitations of human annotations and Named Entity Recognition Systems (NERS) in evaluating relevance, and proposes the use of Large Language Models with Relevance and Judgment Systems (LLM-RJS) to improve relevance ranking. The document highlights the "short-sightedness" of human annotations and NERS, which can fail to identify relevant information that is not immediately obvious. It presents the results of two experiments, showing that LLM-RJS can outperform NERS and human annotations in identifying relevant documents, particularly in cases where the relevance is not immediately apparent. The document also provides examples of cases where LLM-RJS correctly identified relevance, while human annotations and NERS did not. Overall, the document suggests that the addition of reasoning to LLM-RJS can help to overcome the limitations of human annotations and NERS, and improve the accuracy of relevance ranking.

structure.title: Limitations
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the limitations and future work of Large Language Models with Reasoning and Judgment Systems (LLM-RJS) in Information Retrieval tasks. The main points covered include the added cost of LLM-RJS systems compared to embedding models, the scalability issue of LLM-RJS, and the potential solution of using CoT re-ranking. The document also highlights the short-sightedness of human annotations and the potential of LLM-RJS models to outperform human annotations. Additionally, it discusses the need to re-specify the tasks of Information Retrieval problems and how to evaluate LLM-RJS models. The document concludes that LLM-RJS models can address the short-sightedness limitation of NERS models, but human annotations also suffer from the same limitation.

structure.title: Future work
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the limitations and future work of LLM-RJS (Large Language Model - Relevance Judgment Systems) in the context of Information Retrieval. The main points covered include the added cost of LLM-RJS systems compared to embedding models, the potential solution of using CoT re-ranking to address scalability issues, and the short-sightedness of human annotations. The document also highlights the need for re-annotation to establish the performance difference between human relevance and LLM-RJS relevance judgments. Additionally, it touches on the variety of retrieval requirements beyond 'relevance' and the potential of LLM-RJS models to consider soft requirements and deviate from 'similarity' or quantifiable requirements. The document concludes by emphasizing the importance of evaluating LLM-RJS models against human annotations and re-specifying the tasks of Information Retrieval problems.

structure.title: References
structure.summary: The partial document appears to be a collection of research papers and references related to information retrieval, natural language processing, and large language models. The main points covered include relevance assessment, language models, deep learning, and document retrieval techniques. The document also includes a prompt for an automatic passage relevance judge, which is tasked with rating the relevance of a passage to a given query using a 4-point scale. The references cited in the document span from 1965 to 2025, indicating a comprehensive overview of the topic over the years. Key concepts mentioned include few-shot learning, pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers, and the use of large language models for relevance judgment. Overall, the document seems to be focused on the development and evaluation of models for information retrieval and natural language understanding.

structure.title: Appendix A
structure.summary: The partial document describes the task and instructions for an automatic passage relevance judge for information retrieval. The main points covered include carefully reading a query and passage, deciding on relevance, and assigning a score using a 4-point scale: 3 for perfectly relevant, 2 for highly relevant, 1 for related, and 0 for irrelevant, with examples provided for each score.

nodes.title: Prompt for LLMs
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of automatic passage relevance judges for information retrieval, specifically focusing on the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs) with and without reasoning capabilities. It provides a 4-point scale for rating passage relevance to a given query, along with examples and explanations for each score. The document also presents experimental results, including tables and figures, to compare the performance of LLMs with human annotations, highlighting instances where LLMs incorrectly assigned high relevance scores to passages. Additionally, it explores the differences in performance between LLMs with and without reasoning capabilities, noting that LLMs tend to assign higher relevance ratings than humans, especially when using reasoning models.

structure.title: Appendix B
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of language models, specifically GPT 5.1, in terms of their ability to provide relevant responses to queries. It highlights instances of "false positives" where the model assigns high relevance ratings to passages that human annotators deem irrelevant. The document also explores the impact of incorporating reasoning into the model, comparing the performance of GPT 5.1 with and without reasoning, as well as an open-source reasoning model. Additionally, it touches on various topics such as biology, airport codes, lipopolysaccharides, and historical events like the US entry into World War I, which are used as examples to test the model's performance.

nodes.title: Genuine false Positives in GPT 5.1 with reasoning
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of language models, specifically GPT 5.1, in terms of their ability to provide relevant responses to queries. It highlights instances of "false positives" where the model assigns high relevance ratings to passages that human annotators deem irrelevant. The document also explores the impact of incorporating reasoning into the model, comparing the performance of GPT 5.1 with and without reasoning, as well as an open-source reasoning model. The main points covered include the analysis of false positives, the comparison of human and model ratings, and the effects of reasoning on model performance.

nodes.title: Reasoning experiment with GPT-OSS
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the performance of language models, specifically GPT 5.1, in terms of false positives and relevance ratings. It highlights instances where the model gave higher relevance ratings than human annotations, and vice versa. The document also explores the use of reasoning models, such as GPT-OSS, and their impact on relevance ratings. Additionally, it provides examples of failures in language models, including LLM-RJS, where the models failed to provide accurate relevance scores or provided unnecessary justifications. The main points covered include the evaluation of language models, the role of reasoning in relevance ratings, and the analysis of model failures.

structure.title: Appendix D
structure.summary: The partial document discusses examples of LLM-RJS failure, where models fail to comply with the prompt by providing additional justification instead of only giving a relevance score. The document includes three examples of such failures, each with a query, passage, and LLM output, along with a relevance score and justification. The examples cover topics such as the anthropological definition of environment, the cost of interior concrete flooring, and the growth rate of goldfish. The document highlights the models' inability to follow instructions and provide direct answers, instead providing contextual information or related but not directly relevant information.

nodes.title: Examples of LLM-RJS failure
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses examples of LLM-RJS failure, where models fail to provide a direct relevance score and instead offer additional justification. The document includes three examples: the first discusses the relationship between humans and the environment from an anthropological perspective, but fails to provide a definition of "environment". The second example provides information on the cost of interior concrete flooring, but includes unnecessary details. The third example discusses the growth rate of dolphin fish, but fails to directly answer the query about goldfish growth. The document highlights the importance of assigning a relevance score and providing minimal justification, as required by the prompt.
